flashcards.front: Write a short note on APTHOUS SMC STOMATITIS.
flashcards.back:
It is a common disease characterised by development of or painful, recurrant,
solitary or multiple ulcerations of the oral mucasa where the ulcers heal
spontaneously and there are no signs of any other disease.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of APTHOUS SMC STOMATITIS?
flashcards.back:
It has been considered as an idiopathic disease and is associated with
many predispasing factors

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ACCORDING TO MORPHOLOGY.
flashcards.back:
1> Minor Apthae - also called as canker sores in which the ulcers are
oval shaped, less than 1 cm in diameter, and heal
without scar
2> Major Apthae - also called as Sutton's disease or Periadenitis
mucasa necrotica recurrens and the ulcers are
oval shaped, more than 1 cm in diameter and heal
with scarring
3> Herpetiform ulcers - Recurrant crops of dozens of small ulcers which
are less than 0.5 cm are seen throughout oral mucasa

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ACCORDING TO SEVERITY.
flashcards.back:
1> Simple Apthasis - Recurrent attacks of any marphology with
distinct ulcer-free periods
2> Complex Apthasis - Almost constant presence of 3 or more than 3
oral ulcers

flashcards.back: It usually occurs in 2nd and 3rd decades of Life.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on PREVALENCE.
flashcards.back:
Submandibular ducts or glands - 80-94%
Paroted 5-18%
Sublingual n2%
The most common site is submandibular gland and Wharton's duct,
because its secretion is more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course & smaller orifice
It usually occurs in 3rd-5th decades, with slight male predominance
Simultaneous siablithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare
However, multiple sialoliths in the same gland or duct is common

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of PREVALENCE.
flashcards.back:

gland swelling
Insome cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on HERPES ZOSTER.
flashcards.back:
Herpes zaster is an acute infectious viral disease of an extremely
painful and incapacitating nature, characterised by inflammation
of dorsal root ganglion or extramedullary cranial nerve ganglia,
associated with vescicular eruptions of skin and mucus membrane
of the area supplied by affected sensary nerve.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ORAL MANIFESTATIONS.
flashcards.back:
Herpes zaster may involve the face by the infection of trigeminal nerve.
This usually consists of unilateral involvement of skin areas supplied by
either opthalmic, maxillary or mandibular nerves
Lesions of oral mucasa are extremely painful and may be found on the
palate, buccal mucasa, tongue, uvula, pharynx.
After several days of symptoms, intact vescicles appear which soon rupture
to leave areas of erasion of 1-5 mm with exforiation of teeth

flashcards.back:

multinucleated giant cells & intranuclear inclusions [BIOPSY]
However this does not differentiate between herpes zaster & herpes
simplex.
This can only be done by fluorescent antibody staining Technique
or serological diagnosis.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on HERPES SIMPLEX.
flashcards.back:
Herpes simplex virus infections are common vescicular and ulcerative
lesions of mucasa and skin.
Most common intraoral herpetic lesions occur due to HSV-1 while HSV-2
primarily affects the genitals.
Herpetic to infections of oral cavity are of 2 types
A> Primary Herpetic Gingivastomatitis
B> Recurrent Herpetic Stomatitis /Herpes Labialis

flashcards.front: Write a short note on HERPES LABIALIS.
flashcards.back:
Recurrent Herpetic stomatitis is usually seen in adult patients and
manifests itself clinically as an attenuated form of primary disease.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
flashcards.back:
Predisposing factors for reactivation include fever, upper respiratory
tract infection, Trauma and immunasuppression, fatigue, menstruation
and pregnancy, allergy or gastrointestinal disturbances.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
flashcards.back:
Secondary herpes is the result of reactivation of Patent virus.
The viruses, once introduced in the body, appear to reside dormantly
within the regional ganglia, and when reactivation is Triggered, spread
along the nerves to the sites on oral thucasa and skin where they
destroy the epithelial cells and induce a typical nflammatory response.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
flashcards.back:
The lesions are frequently preceded by a burning artingling sensation
and vescicles develop subsequently.
Recurrent intraoral herpetic Lesions almost invariably develop on the
oral mucosa that is tightly bound to periasteum such as palatal mucasa
and attached gingiva, especially in immuno-competent hast
In case of immunocompromised hast, the ulcers may OCCLIR bothat

flashcards.front: Write a short note on MUMPS.
flashcards.back:
It is also called as epidemic parotitis.
Mumps is an acute contagious viral infection characterised by
unilateral or bilateral swelling of salivary glands usually the
parotid.
Other than salivary glands, it may also involve nerve, meninges,
pancreas, gonads.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of MUMPS?
flashcards.back:
It is caused by RNA virus which belongs to family paramyxoviridae
and is found in saliva and urine
It spreads by droplet infection or direct contact

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of MUMPS.
flashcards.back:
Once transmitted through respiratory droplet saliva, urine or
fomites, the incubation period is usually 2-3 weeks.
It starts replicating in the respiratory epithelium and spreads to
Local Lymph nodes, and subsequently develops viraemia
The affected area shows perivascular and interstitial mononuclear
cell infiltrates with oedema
Necrasis of acinar and epithelial duct cells is seen in salivary glands
and in the germinal epithelium of seminiferous tubules.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of MUMPS.
flashcards.back:
Predominant in childhood, more common in boys , age group 5-15 yrs
The disease is preceded by the onset of headache, chills, moderate
fever, vomitting and pain below the ear [ prodromal symptoms]
It is followed by firm, rubbery swelling of salivary glands, which
elevates the ear lasting for 1 week
Salivary gland involvement produces pain upon mastication
Pain and Tenderness may be severe during the rapid phase of
parotid enlargement, the swelling reaches its maximum in about
3 days, tremains at ...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ORAL SUBMUCUS FIBROSIS.
flashcards.back:
OSMF is defined as an insidious chronic disease affecting any part
of the oral cavity and sometimes the pharynx.
It is always associated with juxta-epithelial inflammatory reaction
followed by fibroelastic changes of the Lamina propria with epithe-
Lial atrophy leading to stiffness of oral mucasa and causing trismus
and inability to speak.
The term OSMF was coined by Dr. Jashi in 1953.

It is also known as oral thrush and is one of the most common forms of

It mainly occurs in chronically ill patients and in patients receiving

occuring on buccal mucosa and tongue.

It is established that CHC is a cause of Leukoplakia and thus it has
a premalignant potential.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on RADIATION MUCOSITIS.
flashcards.back:
It is the inflammation of oral mucasal membrane following radiothera-
It develops towards the end of 1st week of therapy and occurs if radiation -py.
given in excess of 3500-4000 rads.
Mucasitis occurs when the rate of epithelial growth and repair are
affected by radiation, resulting in epithelial thinning erasion and
ulceration

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of RADIATION MUCOSITIS.

Mast commonly found in children and elderly adults with male

Redness of oral mucasa followed by pseudomembrane formation with

flashcards.front: Write a short note on VISUAL ANALOG SCALE.
flashcards.back:
ТИ.
It is a measurement instrument that tries to measure a characte-
- ristic or attitude that is believed to range across a continuum
from none to an extreme amount of pain.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on METHOD.
flashcards.back:
Ask the patient to indicate on the Line where the pain is in relation
to the 2 extremes.
Ask the patient to mark on the Line the point that he feels his
perception of his current state.
The VAS scare is determined by measuring in mm from the Left
hand end of the Line to the point that the patient marks.
When the VAS is properly explained to subjects, they can easily
understand its use and successfully use it to indicate his level
of pain response to hypersensitive stimuli.

flashcards.back:
A disorder of the trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing
pain in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face.
People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal
neuralgia gets its French name " Tic doucloureux" meaning painful
twitch.

trigeminal neuralgia nerve at the base of the brain

flashcards.front: Write a short note on MACROGLOSSIA.
flashcards.back:
Macroglassia is a retiv relatively common condition characterised by
an abnormally large tongue in the oral cavity.

flashcards.front: What are the types of MACROGLOSSIA?
flashcards.back:
Macroglassia can be either congenital or acquired [secondary] in
nature
Relative Macroglassia - normal sized tongue appears abnormally
large, if it is enclosed in a small oral cavity.
It happens mostly in cases of maxillary retrusion or in cases of
restricted growth of nasopharynx
Apparant Macroglassia - inthis, tongue appears abnormally large due
to poor muscular control of the tongue, although there is no real increase
in tongue size.

flashcards.front: Describe MACROGLOSSIA.
flashcards.back:
Congenital (BCD LMN H²V)
Acquired (A³MCE)

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of MACROGLOSSIA.
flashcards.back:
Macroglossia is most prominent in infants but tongue size may remain
above normal in childhood and adolescence.
It cause displacement of teeth and malocclusion due To continuous pressure
on the Teeth by enlarged tongue.
Disturbs the process of speech or food intake
Lateral margin of tongue exhibits scalloping or Indentations
Blockage of the pharyngeal airway due to macroglossia may result in
a, condition called obstruction sleep apnea which is characterised by
intermittent cessation of respirati...

flashcards.front: Describe the management of MACROGLOSSIA.
flashcards.back:
Orofacial therapy - it uses a palatal device to stimulate muscular
tone and proper tongue position IL
Surgical management - partial glassectomy is the Treatment of
choice.
Removal of the primary cause, wherever necessary at
Orthodontic treatment - correction of dental arch deformity &
malocclusion by arthodontic treatment
Speech therapy - correction of defective articulation by speech therapy

It is also called Lingua villasa, Lingua nigra.

It is a common disease characterised by development of or painful, recurrant,
solitary or multiple ulcerations of the oral mucasa where the ulcers heal
spontaneously and there are no signs of any other disease

It has been considered as an idiopathic disease and is associated with
many predispasing factors

α-hemolytic
binding of IgG &
streptococci,
Asthma,
IgM, mucasal

1> Minor Apthae - also called as canker sores in which the ulcers are
oval shaped, less than 1 cm in diameter, and heal
without scar
2> Major Apthae - also called as Sutton's disease or Periadenitis
mucasa necrotica recurrens and the ulcers are
oval shaped, more than 1 cm in diameter and heal
with scarring
3> Herpetiform ulcers - Recurrant crops of dozens of small ulcers which
are less than 0
5 cm are seen throughout oral mucasa

1> Simple Apthasis - Recurrent attacks of any marphology with
distinct ulcer-free periods
2> Complex Apthasis - Almost constant presence of 3 or more than 3
oral ulcers
It usually occurs in 2nd and 3rd decades of Life

It occurs most commonly in nonkeratinised mucasa such as buccal
and Labial mucasa tongue, soft palate, gingiva
Prodromal symptoms - prodromal burning itching for 24-48 hours,
before the ulcer appears

The lesion is typically very painful, so it commonly interferes with
eating for several days
It begins as a single or multiple superficial erasions covered by gray
membrane

It is surrounded by localised areas of erythema and develops within
hours
Multiple lesions are present but the number and size varies

The ulcers generally persist for 7-14 days, may recur after 1-4
months with no evidence of scarring

A> TOPICAL MANAGEMENT
Topical carticasteroid - 1% triamcinolone acetonide 3-4 times daily

Other topical steroids are betamethasone syrup
and 0
01 % dexamethasone elixir

Topical Anaesthetics - 2% viscous lidocaine, benzocaine and benzydamine
hydrochlaride may reduce pain

Sucralfate - Topical application 4 times/day has shown soothing
effect on ulcer as it sticks to the mucus membrane

Sialolithiasis is the formation of sialolith [salivary calculi] in the salivary
duct or gland resulting in the mechanical obstruction of the salivary flow

Dehydration - increases the viscasity and concentration of saliva
Reduced food intake / fasting / anarexia
sull
- decreases the demand for saliva, can bring about stasis of saliva
Certain drugs which Lower the production of saliva
- antihistamines, antihypertensives, diuretics ,antidepressants
Lack of saliva in certain conditions such as
- Sjogrens Syndrome, Xerastomia, tobacco smoking, hypercalcemia

A> Ductal Sialolithiasis
B> Glandular Sialolithiasis

Submandibular ducts or glands - 80-94%
Paroted 5-18%
Sublingual n2%
The most common site is submandibular gland and Wharton's duct,
because its secretion is more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course & smaller orifice
It usually occurs in 3rd-5th decades, with slight male predominance
Simultaneous siablithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare
However, multiple sialoliths in the same gland or duct is common

Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the 2nd most common
salivary gland disease in children [ after mumps]
It is the major cause of unilateral, diffuse parotid or submandibular
gland swelling
Insome cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever

The treatment depends on the number, size and Location of sialolith

If the sialolith is small and single, conservative management with
local heat, massage and sialogoques is attempted
In case of infection, initially antibiotics and anti-inflammatory kitaral
medication and enhanced use of sialogogues, salivary gland massage
and hyperhydration hoping for sportaneous passage is given
Techniques for removal of sialolith are -
A> Manual manipulation with the help of Lacrimal probes or dilators
[called as milking the gland ] - for sialoliths less than 2 cm, which
are Located peripherally near the ductal opening
B> Larger sialoliths in the anterior part are surgically removed,
by sialolithectomy, through a ductotomy, performed intraarally,
under LA
Posterior stones are removed under GA

Herpes zaster is an acute infectious viral disease of an extremely
painful and incapacitating nature, characterised by inflammation
of dorsal root ganglion or extramedullary cranial nerve ganglia,
associated with vescicular eruptions of skin and mucus membrane
of the area supplied by affected sensary nerve

revision_nodes.topic: It is also called as SHINGLES or ZONA
key_points:
strength
The word zaster comes from the Latin for belt, as the lesion occurs in
belt- Like distribution

causative agent' - varicella zaster virus
Triggering factors are - trauma, development of malignancy,
Tumor involvement of dorsal root ganglia, local X-ray radiation
or immunosuppressive therapy
Herpes zaster is caused by reactivation of Latent V-Z virus,
which had been acquired during a previous attack of chickenpox
<01

АИД (a
Although rare, it does occur in children under 15 years of age
Affects males and females with equal frequency
Initial symptoms are fever, malaise, pain and tenderness along the
course of involved sensory nerves, usually unilaterally

In a few days the patient has a Linear papular or vescicular eruption
of the skin or mucasa supplied by the affected nerves

A zone of erythema surrounds the vescicle, giving a dew drop on
a rose petal" appearance

After the rupture of vescicles, healing commences by scabbing,
although secondary infection may intervene and slow the process
considerably

Hutchinson sign - It is cutaneous zaster infection of the side of the
nase
Herpes zaster may involve the face by the infection of trigeminal nerve

This usually consists of unilateral involvement of skin areas supplied by
either opthalmic, maxillary or mandibular nerves
Lesions of oral mucasa are extremely painful and may be found on the
palate, buccal mucasa, tongue, uvula, pharynx

After several days of symptoms, intact vescicles appear which soon rupture
to leave areas of erasion of 1-5 mm with exforiation of teeth
Trigeminal herpes zaster occuring during tooth formation causes
pulpal necrasis and internal root resorption, exfoliation of teeth
So, in the case of herpes zaster, several non-vital teeth can be seen

Oral lesions can be easily identified by identifying characteristic
multinucleated giant cells & intranuclear inclusions [BIOPSY]
However this does not differentiate between herpes zaster & herpes
simplex

This can only be done by fluorescent antibody staining Technique
or serological diagnosis

Antiviral drugs (1st Line therapy) Losso
out
of
- should be initiated within 72 hours for maximum effectiveness
- They block viral replication & accelerate healing in the acute
phase of illness

- A delay in administration may result in neuronal destruction
leading to neuropathic pain syndrome associated with past-
out
herpetic neuralgia

Supportive care, electrolytes, a Liquid diet, and nutritional support
should be started as early as possible
If drug is the causative factor, discontinue the drug up
If patient is dehydrated due to pain, then IV rehydration should be
carried out
Topical anaesthetic agents (lidocaine gel) antiseptic mouthwashes with
hydrogen peroxide or chlorhexidine & topical carticasteroids to reduce
discomfort

Far minor EM, 20-40mg/day prednisolone is administered for 4-6
days and then taper the dose

Major EM usually requires 40-80
mg/day with gradual tapering over 2-3 weeks

key_points:
Recurrent Herpetic stomatitis is usually seen in adult patients and
manifests itself clinically as an attenuated form of primary disease

Predisposing factors for reactivation include fever, upper respiratory
tract infection, Trauma and immunasuppression, fatigue, menstruation
and pregnancy, allergy or gastrointestinal disturbances
Secondary herpes is the result of reactivation of Patent virus

The viruses, once introduced in the body, appear to reside dormantly
within the regional ganglia, and when reactivation is Triggered, spread
along the nerves to the sites on oral thucasa and skin where they
destroy the epithelial cells and induce a typical nflammatory response

The lesions are frequently preceded by a burning artingling sensation
and vescicles develop subsequently

Recurrent intraoral herpetic Lesions almost invariably develop on the
oral mucosa that is tightly bound to periasteum such as palatal mucasa
and attached gingiva, especially in immuno-competent hast
In case of immunocompromised hast, the ulcers may OCCLIR bothat

Recurrent Herpes Labialis - most Lesions appear on vermillion of Lip
and surrounding skin, they are gray or white vescicles which rupture
quickly leaving small red ulcerations, sometimes with slight erythe-
matous halo on covered by brownish crust on Lips

Size ranges from 1-3 mm to 1-2 cT, rarely can be Large enough
to cause dissigurement
Past history of recurrent herpes Labialis

and a positive history of clase
contact with a patient with primary or recurrent herpes is helpful in
making diagnasis
Serological tests such as complement fixation, RIA & ELISA

5% acyclovir, 3% perciclovir and 10% docasanol are applied 4-6times
per day at the first prodrome

This will reduce the severity of lesion
Recurrent Herpetic Labialis can be suppressed by reducing trigger
factor such as by applying sunscreen at least 1/2 hour before sun
exposure

Systematically acyclovir (400mg BD for 4 months) and valacyclovir
(500 mg- 1g OD for 4 -months) as preventive therapy has shown to
increase the time interval between 2 recurrent episodes

Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva,
as a result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts

It is not a true cyst as it Lacks an epithelial lining
If
There are 2 types of mucoceles
A> Extravasation type
B> Retention type
These 2 types differi in the pathogenesis

The extravasation type is the
common one

A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
of
It is caused by trauma leading to Laceration of the duct, commonly of
the minar salivary glands, resulting in extravasation of mucin into
connective tissue

The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of
the Lip by extraction forceps

sult
The mucin, which extravasates into connective tissue, leads to infla-
mmotion, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site,
which in turn result in the formation of granulation tissue,
which actually forms the wall of these extravasation cysts

B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because
of a sialolith or may be due to periductal scar, or impinging tumar,
resulting in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct
This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst Like lesion

sinus

Mumps is an acute contagious viral infection characterised by
unilateral or bilateral swelling of salivary glands usually the
parotid

Other than salivary glands, it may also involve nerve, meninges,
pancreas, gonads

It is caused by RNA virus which belongs to family paramyxoviridae
and is found in saliva and urine
It spreads by droplet infection or direct contact

Once transmitted through respiratory droplet saliva, urine or
fomites, the incubation period is usually 2-3 weeks

It starts replicating in the respiratory epithelium and spreads to
Local Lymph nodes, and subsequently develops viraemia
The affected area shows perivascular and interstitial mononuclear
cell infiltrates with oedema
Necrasis of acinar and epithelial duct cells is seen in salivary glands
and in the germinal epithelium of seminiferous tubules

Predominant in childhood, more common in boys , age group 5-15 yrs
The disease is preceded by the onset of headache, chills, moderate
fever, vomitting and pain below the ear [ prodromal symptoms]
It is followed by firm, rubbery swelling of salivary glands, which
elevates the ear lasting for 1 week
Salivary gland involvement produces pain upon mastication
Pain and Tenderness may be severe during the rapid phase of
parotid enlargement, the swelling reaches its maximum in about
3 days, tremains at its peak for 2-3 days and then subsides
gradually

Most cases are unrecognised because of the absence of parotid swelling
The submandibular gland may also be involved separately or
in conjunction with the parotid gland

OSMF is defined as an insidious chronic disease affecting any part
of the oral cavity and sometimes the pharynx

It is always associated with juxta-epithelial inflammatory reaction
followed by fibroelastic changes of the Lamina propria with epithe-
Lial atrophy leading to stiffness of oral mucasa and causing trismus
and inability to speak
The term OSMF was coined by Dr

Most common precancerous lesion of Southeast Asian countries &
particularly Indian subcontinent

A> LOCAL FACTORS
Excessive areca nut chewing [single most important factor]
- Because it contains chemicals such as arecoline, arecaidine
which stimulates the fibroblast cells of Lamina propria &
produce excessive amount of collagen

B> ADVANCED OSMF
As the disease progresses, the oral mucasa becomes blanched and
slightly opaque and white fibrous bands appear
The density of fibrous deposit varies from a slightly whitish area
on the soft palate, causing no symptoms, to a dense fibrasis, causing
fixation or shartening or deviation of the uvula and soft palate
Gradual stiffening of oral mucasa with Loss of tissue resiliency,
fibrasis of pterygomandibular region often leads to trismus
Impairment CNI of tongue movement with atrophy of tongue papilla
When the fibrasis involves the nasopharynx, the patient may
experience referred pain to the ear
In extreme cases, uvula gives hockey-stick or bud like appearance

It is a measurement instrument that tries to measure a characte-
- ristic or attitude that is believed to range across a continuum
from none to an extreme amount of pain

Ask the patient to indicate on the Line where the pain is in relation
to the 2 extremes

Ask the patient to mark on the Line the point that he feels his
perception of his current state

The VAS scare is determined by measuring in mm from the Left
hand end of the Line to the point that the patient marks

When the VAS is properly explained to subjects, they can easily
understand its use and successfully use it to indicate his level
of pain response to hypersensitive stimuli

membrane

Macroglassia is a retiv relatively common condition characterised by
an abnormally large tongue in the oral cavity

Macroglassia can be either congenital or acquired [secondary] in
nature
Relative Macroglassia - normal sized tongue appears abnormally
large, if it is enclosed in a small oral cavity

It happens mostly in cases of maxillary retrusion or in cases of
restricted growth of nasopharynx
Apparant Macroglassia - inthis, tongue appears abnormally large due
to poor muscular control of the tongue, although there is no real increase
in tongue size

Congenital (BCD LMN H²V)
Acquired (A³MCE)

Macroglossia is most prominent in infants but tongue size may remain
above normal in childhood and adolescence

It cause displacement of teeth and malocclusion due To continuous pressure
on the Teeth by enlarged tongue

Disturbs the process of speech or food intake
Lateral margin of tongue exhibits scalloping or Indentations
Blockage of the pharyngeal airway due to macroglossia may result in
a, condition called obstruction sleep apnea which is characterised by
intermittent cessation of respiration during sleep

Edentulous patients with macroglassia often have difficulty in wearing
dentures
Patient may complain of noisy breathing drooling of saliva

Orofacial therapy - it uses a palatal device to stimulate muscular
tone and proper tongue position IL
Surgical management - partial glassectomy is the Treatment of
choice

Removal of the primary cause, wherever necessary at
Orthodontic treatment - correction of dental arch deformity &
malocclusion by arthodontic treatment
Speech therapy - correction of defective articulation by speech therapy

the treatment of cancer results in pronounced changes in the salivary
glands Located within the primary beam

volume factor

cells

can also Lead to xerastomia

-une states

Pagets disease of bone is a condition characterised by abnormal
resorption and deposition of bone resulting in distartion and
weakening of affected bones
It is also called as asteitis deformans

The cause of Pagets disease is unknown
It may be caused by virus (Paramyxoviridae)

Others - vasculitis, trauma, harmonal imbalance and degenerative
neurologic disorders

Affects men twice as frequently as women
Mainly occurs in the axial skeleton, especially the skull, femur,
sacrum and pelvis

Major symptoms include skull enlargement, Bone pain, deafness
Simian appearance, bowing of legs may be present

development of oral cancer

- smoking

N stands for Lymph nodes

Size of Lesion < 2 CM

N2A Metastasis to a single ipsilateral LN > 3cm but diameter < 6cm
N2B Metastasis in multiple ipsilateral LN < 6cm
N2C Metastasis in bilateralar contralateral nodes, < 6cm
N3 Metastasis in a LN more than 6 cm

long_essay.question: Describe the management of PREVALENCE.
long_essay.model_answer:
The treatment depends on the number, size and Location of sialolith.
If the sialolith is small and single, conservative management with
local heat, massage and sialogoques is attempted
In case of infection, initially antibiotics and anti-inflammatory kitaral
medication and enhanced use of sialogogues, salivary gland massage
and hyperhydration hoping for sportaneous passage is given
Techniques for removal of sialolith are -
A> Manual manipulation with the help of Lacrimal probes or dilators
[called as milking the gland ] - for sialoliths less than 2 cm, which
are Located peripherally near the ductal opening
B> Larger sialoliths in the anterior part are surgically removed,
by sialolithectomy, through a ductotomy, performed intraarally,
under LA. Posterior stones are removed under GA.
c> Ductal Cannulation
beels
D> Multiple stones or stones in the gland very posterior brolp stones,
failed transoral route, require the removal of the gland.
)
Extraoral approach- GA.
bring
E> Newer con-surgical approach
- Lithotripsy [ to fragment the salivary stones ]
It is of 2 types - intracarpareal
- extracarpareal

1> Candidiasis - It is the most common oral manifestation of HIV.
ALL the three types i.e pseudomembranous erythematous, and hyperplastic
forms are seen.
Erythematous form is seen when CD 4+ count drops below 400/mm3 and
pseudomembranous develop when CD 4+ count drop below 200/mm3.
2> Oral Hairy Leukoplakia - Presence of soft painless plaque on the
lateral barder of tongue with carreigated surface Caused by EB virus.
3> Kapasi's Sarcoma - It is a multifocal neoplasm of vascular endothelial
origin. Most common tumor associated with AIDS.
Human Herpes Virus type 8 is involved in the pathogenesis of Kapasis
sarcoma.
commonly affected sites are the palate, gingiva and Tongue
4> Non-Hodgekins Lymphoma - It is the second most common cancer
associated with AIDS.
The lesion Tends to present as a Large, painful, ulcerated mass on the
palate or gingival tissues.

5> Linear Gingival Erythema - It is a non-plaque induced gingivitis
exhibiting a distinct erythematous band of marginal gingiva with
either diffused or punctuated erythema of the attached gingiva.
6> Necrotising Ulcerative Gingivitis F NUG involves ulceration and
necrasis of one or more interdental papilla and is Limited to the marginal
gingiva
7> Necrotising Ulcerative Periodontitis - NUP extends beyond the papilla
and marginal gingiva causing Loss of periodontal attachment,
exposing the bone.

long_essay.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUCOCELE.
long_essay.model_answer:
A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
of
It is caused by trauma leading to Laceration of the duct, commonly of
the minar salivary glands, resulting in extravasation of mucin into
connective tissue.
The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of
the Lip by extraction forceps.
sult
The mucin, which extravasates into connective tissue, leads to infla-
mmotion, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site,
which in turn result in the formation of granulation tissue,
which actually forms the wall of these extravasation cysts.
B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because
of a sialolith or may be due to periductal scar, or impinging tumar,
resulting in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct.
11.
This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst Like lesion.
These cysts may show the presence of ductal epithelium histologically.

B> SUPPORTIVE THERAPY

c> MEDICINAL THERAPY
1> STEROIDS
Local - Hydrocartisone injection along with procaine hydrochloride
injection locally in the area of fibrasis.
Injections are given fortnightly.
Systemic - A therapy with hydrocartisone tablet 25 mg, in doses of
100 mg /day is useful in relieving burning sensation
Triamcinolone or 90mg Dexamethasone can be given
2> PLACENTAL EXTRACTS
Placentrax is an essential biogenic stimulator
It accelerates cellular metabolism, stimulates regenerative process,
produces significant enhancement of wound healing

[suoital
It breaks down hyaluronic acid and prevents collagen formation
biro
Thus it improves the health of mucus membrane, burning sensation
4> LYCOPENE
Antioxidant, Tab 2000 ug for 3 months
5> GAMMA INTERFERON
Intralesional injection of r-interferon improves mouth opening
and reduces mucasal burning
Pentoxifylline - 400 mg 3timely daily
D> SURGICAL METHODS
It is the method of choice when there is marked limitation in mouth
opening and when biopsy reveals neoplastic changes.

long_essay.question: Describe the clinical features of Radiation therapy.
long_essay.model_answer:
1> PSEUDOMEMBRANOUS
It is also known as oral thrush and is one of the most common forms of
candidiasis
It mainly occurs in chronically ill patients and in patients receiving
systemic carticasteroid therapy
Oral lesions are soft, white slightly elevated plaques most frequently
occuring on buccal mucosa and tongue.
The white plaque can usually be wiped away with a gauze leaving
either a relatively normal appearing mucasa or an erythematous
area.
2> ERYTHEMATOUS
flaking lips
Also known as antibiotic sore mouth, it includes central papillary
atrophy of tongue and cheilocandidiasis.
The lesions appear red or erythematous and redness is due to increased
vascularity.
It is the only variety of oral candidiasis, which is consistently painful.
Erythematous appearance is due to Loss of filiform papillae
3> CHRONIC HYPERPLASTIC

long_essay.question: Describe the management of Immunoflorescence studies.
long_essay.model_answer:
The initial aim of the treatment is to induce disease remission.
This should be followed by a period of maintenance treatment,
using the minimum drug doses required for disease control
in order To minimise their side effects.
a> Topical Therapy - Pts. with mild mucasal Lesions can be managed
by topical therapy alone.
Soft diets and soft toothbrushes help minimise Local trauma
Topical analgesics or anaesthetics eg - benzydamine hydrochlaride
oral rinse are useful.
Antiseptic mouthwashes, such as chlorhexidine gluconate 0.2%
b> Topical Steroids - Mouth rinse of betamethasone sodium phasphate
0.5 mg tablet in 10 ml water tds
Triamcinolone acetonide 0.1% in adhesive paste.
Topical CS therapy may help reduce the requirement of systemic agents
c> Systemic CS - Best therapy for management of PV
On average; cessation of blistering takes 2-3 weeks and full healing
takes 6-8 weeks
Patients are treated with initial prednisolone 40-60mg/day and in
If no response within 5-7 days, dase should be increased in also 50-100%
severe cases 60-100 mg/day

long_essay.question: Write a short note on Painful salivary gland enlargement.
long_essay.model_answer:
Effect on Oral Functions - increased thirst, increased uptake of
fluid especially while eating difficulty in swallowing, speech, and
eating dry food. There is also burning and tingling sensation in
the mouth, frequent oral infections, intolerance to dental
appliances and abnormal taste in the mouth.
Effect on Normal functions - hypofunction of other secretary
glands, blurred vision and ocular dryness, itching and burning
sensation in the eye. There is also dryness of pharynx and skin
Residual saliva is form foamy, thick and ropey.
Clinical signs - oral mucasa appears thin, pale and dry. Tongue blade
may adhere to soft tissues. Tongue may manifest deficiency by atrophy
of the papilla, inf lammation fissuring cracking and denudation.
There is also increased incidence of dental caries.
Presence of pseudomembranous and hyperplastic candidiasis

long_essay.question: Write a short note on RISK FACTORS FOR ORAL CANCER.
long_essay.model_answer:
1> TOBACCO USE - Tobacco use is the major etiological factor for the
development of oral cancer.
The effect of tobacco use heavy alchohol consumption and poor diet
can probably explain 90% of cases of oral cancer.
Tobacco is used in 2 major farms
- smokeless tobacco
- smoking
Tobacco contains potent carcinogens Like nitrosamine, polycylic
hydrocarbons, polonium etc

Tobacco smoke contains CO, which is an important factor in the
development of oral cancer
2> ALCHOHOL - ALL forms of alchohol including hard Liquor, wine and
beer have been implicated in the etiology of oral cancer.
It acts by bits dehydrating effect on mucasa which increases the mucosal
permeability.
3> FAMILIAL AND GENETIC PREAISPOSITION - Though it is considered
as a risk factor, there is Little evidence supporting it.
4> ACTINIC RADIATION - It is a minor etiologic factor in case of lip
cancer
5> ORODENTAL FACTORS It is more prevalant in patients with
poor oral hygiene faulty restorations, sharp Teeth, ill-fitting
dentures and those with syphilitic glassitis
6> VIT. A DEFICIENCY - Vit. A deficiency can produce excessive
keratinisation of skin & HUCUS membrane and, thus may
predispase to carcinoma
7> IMMUNITY - The increasing incidence of oral cancers is clearly
age related, due to decreasing immune response with age.
It may occur in immunocompromised patients following
organ & bone marrow transplantation.
8> SYPHILIS - Oral malignancy is commonly found in Tertiary
stage of syphilis.
9> to IONISING RADIATION - Carcinoma of buccal mucosa may
occur as a complication of long term radiotherapy.
10> TRAUMA - Trauma in combination with other factors TOOD like миг chronic
check biting, denture use and irregularteeth may promote transforma-
-tion of epithelial cells.
11> VIRUS - Viruses such 0 as herpes simplex OF type 1, human papilloma
virus can be associated with oral cancer.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on APTHOUS SMC STOMATITIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is a common disease characterised by development of or painful, recurrant,
solitary or multiple ulcerations of the oral mucasa where the ulcers heal
spontaneously and there are no signs of any other disease.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ACCORDING TO MORPHOLOGY.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> Minor Apthae - also called as canker sores in which the ulcers are
oval shaped, less than 1 cm in diameter, and heal
without scar
2> Major Apthae - also called as Sutton's disease or Periadenitis
mucasa necrotica recurrens and the ulcers are
oval shaped, more than 1 cm in diameter and heal
with scarring
3> Herpetiform ulcers - Recurrant crops of dozens of small ulcers which
are less than 0.5 cm are seen throughout oral mucasa

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Female predilection.
short_answer.model_answer:
It occurs most commonly in nonkeratinised mucasa such as buccal
and Labial mucasa tongue, soft palate, gingiva
Prodromal symptoms - prodromal burning itching for 24-48 hours,
before the ulcer appears.
The lesion is typically very painful, so it commonly interferes with
eating for several days
It begins as a single or multiple superficial erasions covered by gray
membrane.
It is surrounded by localised areas of erythema and develops within
hours.
Multiple lesions are present but the number and size varies.
The ulcers generally persist for 7-14 days, may recur after 1-4
months with no evidence of scarring.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on PREVALENCE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Submandibular ducts or glands - 80-94%
Paroted 5-18%
Sublingual n2%
The most common site is submandibular gland and Wharton's duct,
because its secretion is more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course & smaller orifice
It usually occurs in 3rd-5th decades, with slight male predominance
Simultaneous siablithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare
However, multiple sialoliths in the same gland or duct is common

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of PREVALENCE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the 2nd most common
salivary gland disease in children [ after mumps]
It is the major cause of unilateral, diffuse parotid or submandibular
gland swelling
Insome cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever.
occissociation
IN
with
Patients complain of pain and swelling during and after eating the
food when saliva production is at its peak. There is gradual
reduction of pain & swelling, which occurs at the resting period.
It recurs again repeatedly, when flow is stimulated.
Tenderness, trismus, decrease or absence of salivary flow, or
purulent discharge at the orifice of the duct may be present
Occasisionally, the sialolith presents no remarkable symptoms

short_answer.question: Describe the management of PREVALENCE.
short_answer.model_answer:
The treatment depends on the number, size and Location of sialolith.
If the sialolith is small and single, conservative management with
local heat, massage and sialogoques is attempted
In case of infection, initially antibiotics and anti-inflammatory kitaral
medication and enhanced use of sialogogues, salivary gland massage
and hyperhydration hoping for sportaneous passage is given
Techniques for removal of sialolith are -
A> Manual manipulation with the help of Lacrimal probes or dilators
[called as milking the gland ] - for sialoliths less than 2 cm, which
are Located peripherally near the ductal opening
B> Larger sialoliths in the anterior part are surgically removed,
by sialolithectomy, through a ductotomy, performed intraarally,
under LA. Posterior stones are removed under GA.
c> Ductal Cannulation

failed transoral route, require the removal of the gland.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on HERPES ZOSTER.
short_answer.model_answer:
Herpes zaster is an acute infectious viral disease of an extremely
painful and incapacitating nature, characterised by inflammation
of dorsal root ganglion or extramedullary cranial nerve ganglia,
associated with vescicular eruptions of skin and mucus membrane
of the area supplied by affected sensary nerve.

causative agent' - varicella zaster virus

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Most common in adult Life.
short_answer.model_answer:
АИД (a
Although rare, it does occur in children under 15 years of age
Affects males and females with equal frequency
Initial symptoms are fever, malaise, pain and tenderness along the
course of involved sensory nerves, usually unilaterally.
In a few days the patient has a Linear papular or vescicular eruption
of the skin or mucasa supplied by the affected nerves.
A zone of erythema surrounds the vescicle, giving a dew drop on
a rose petal" appearance.
After the rupture of vescicles, healing commences by scabbing,
although secondary infection may intervene and slow the process
considerably.
Hutchinson sign - It is cutaneous zaster infection of the side of the
nase.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ORAL MANIFESTATIONS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Herpes zaster may involve the face by the infection of trigeminal nerve.
This usually consists of unilateral involvement of skin areas supplied by
either opthalmic, maxillary or mandibular nerves
Lesions of oral mucasa are extremely painful and may be found on the
palate, buccal mucasa, tongue, uvula, pharynx.
After several days of symptoms, intact vescicles appear which soon rupture
to leave areas of erasion of 1-5 mm with exforiation of teeth
Trigeminal herpes zaster occuring during tooth formation causes
pulpal necrasis and internal root resorption, exfoliation of teeth.
So, in the case of herpes zaster, several non-vital teeth can be seen.

short_answer.question: How is ORAL MANIFESTATIONS diagnosed?
short_answer.model_answer:
Oral lesions can be easily identified by identifying characteristic
multinucleated giant cells & intranuclear inclusions [BIOPSY]
However this does not differentiate between herpes zaster & herpes
simplex.
This can only be done by fluorescent antibody staining Technique
or serological diagnosis.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on HERPES SIMPLEX.
short_answer.model_answer:
Herpes simplex virus infections are common vescicular and ulcerative
lesions of mucasa and skin.
Most common intraoral herpetic lesions occur due to HSV-1 while HSV-2
primarily affects the genitals.
Herpetic to infections of oral cavity are of 2 types
A> Primary Herpetic Gingivastomatitis
B> Recurrent Herpetic Stomatitis /Herpes Labialis

short_answer.question: Describe the pathogenesis of PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Secondary herpes is the result of reactivation of Patent virus.
The viruses, once introduced in the body, appear to reside dormantly
within the regional ganglia, and when reactivation is Triggered, spread
along the nerves to the sites on oral thucasa and skin where they
destroy the epithelial cells and induce a typical nflammatory response.

Recurrent intraoral herpetic Lesions almost invariably develop on the

and attached gingiva, especially in immuno-competent hast

resulting in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct.

short_answer.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUMPS.

fomites, the incubation period is usually 2-3 weeks.
It starts replicating in the respiratory epithelium and spreads to
Local Lymph nodes, and subsequently develops viraemia
The affected area shows perivascular and interstitial mononuclear
cell infiltrates with oedema
Necrasis of acinar and epithelial duct cells is seen in salivary glands
and in the germinal epithelium of seminiferous tubules.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of MUMPS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Predominant in childhood, more common in boys , age group 5-15 yrs
The disease is preceded by the onset of headache, chills, moderate
fever, vomitting and pain below the ear [ prodromal symptoms]
It is followed by firm, rubbery swelling of salivary glands, which
elevates the ear lasting for 1 week
Salivary gland involvement produces pain upon mastication
Pain and Tenderness may be severe during the rapid phase of
parotid enlargement, the swelling reaches its maximum in about
3 days, tremains at its peak for 2-3 days and then subsides
gradually.
Most cases are unrecognised because of the absence of parotid swelling
The submandibular gland may also be involved separately or
in conjunction with the parotid gland.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ORAL SUBMUCUS FIBROSIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
OSMF is defined as an insidious chronic disease affecting any part
of the oral cavity and sometimes the pharynx.
It is always associated with juxta-epithelial inflammatory reaction
followed by fibroelastic changes of the Lamina propria with epithe-
Lial atrophy leading to stiffness of oral mucasa and causing trismus
and inability to speak.
The term OSMF was coined by Dr. Jashi in 1953.

short_answer.question: What is the etiology of ORAL SUBMUCUS FIBROSIS?
short_answer.model_answer:
A> LOCAL FACTORS
Excessive areca nut chewing [single most important factor]
- Because it contains chemicals such as arecoline, arecaidine
which stimulates the fibroblast cells of Lamina propria &
produce excessive amount of collagen.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Farmation of petechial spots.
short_answer.model_answer:
B> ADVANCED OSMF
As the disease progresses, the oral mucasa becomes blanched and
slightly opaque and white fibrous bands appear
The density of fibrous deposit varies from a slightly whitish area
on the soft palate, causing no symptoms, to a dense fibrasis, causing
fixation or shartening or deviation of the uvula and soft palate
Gradual stiffening of oral mucasa with Loss of tissue resiliency,
fibrasis of pterygomandibular region often leads to trismus
Impairment CNI of tongue movement with atrophy of tongue papilla
When the fibrasis involves the nasopharynx, the patient may
experience referred pain to the ear.
In extreme cases, uvula gives hockey-stick or bud like appearance.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of Radiation therapy.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> PSEUDOMEMBRANOUS
It is also known as oral thrush and is one of the most common forms of
candidiasis
It mainly occurs in chronically ill patients and in patients receiving
systemic carticasteroid therapy
Oral lesions are soft, white slightly elevated plaques most frequently
occuring on buccal mucosa and tongue.
The white plaque can usually be wiped away with a gauze leaving
either a relatively normal appearing mucasa or an erythematous
area.
2> ERYTHEMATOUS
flaking lips

atrophy of tongue and cheilocandidiasis.
The lesions appear red or erythematous and redness is due to increased
vascularity.
It is the only variety of oral candidiasis, which is consistently painful.
Erythematous appearance is due to Loss of filiform papillae
3> CHRONIC HYPERPLASTIC

short_answer.model_answer:
The oral lesions consist of firm, white, persistant plaques usually
on the Lips tongue, cheeks and appear similar to leukoplakia
It is established that CHC is a cause of Leukoplakia and thus it has
a premalignant potential.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on RADIATION MUCOSITIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is the inflammation of oral mucasal membrane following radiothera-
It develops towards the end of 1st week of therapy and occurs if radiation -py.
given in excess of 3500-4000 rads.
Mucasitis occurs when the rate of epithelial growth and repair are
affected by radiation, resulting in epithelial thinning erasion and
ulceration

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of RADIATION MUCOSITIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Mast commonly found in children and elderly adults with male
predilection
Radiation has more marked effect on rapidly proliferating epithelium
and, therefore, mucositis involves the nonkeratinised mucasa first
Redness of oral mucasa followed by pseudomembrane formation with
large area of oral mucasa covered with grayish-white slough alternating
with areas of more severe ulceration
The first sign of mucasitis may be whitish appearance of the mucasa
due to hyperkeratinisation and intraepithelial edema or a red appearan-
ce due to hyperemia

short_answer.question: Describe the management of RADIATION MUCOSITIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> Mouth Rinse - A soothing mouth rinse such as an antihistaminic

2> Good Oral Hygiene - Once radiation therapy is initiated all foci

3> Betamethasone Mouth wash -
4> Other Therapy - Other therapy like topical allopurinol, antimicro-

Limited success.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of Alchohol.
short_answer.model_answer:
Age - mostly 4th-5th decades of Life
rarely, the lesion occurs below 30 years of age
Sex - gender distribution varies from strong male predominance
in India to 1:1 in western world
Site - most commonly affected is buccal mucasa and surrounding
areas. Tongue, lips, gingiva, palate are also affected
In most of the cases, leukoplakia is asymptomatic, however, in some
cases [non homogenous type] mild pain is recorded.
Size of the lesion may vary from small, well localised patch few
mm in diameter to a diffuse large lesion, covering a wide mucasal
surface.
The surface of the Lesion may be smooth or finely wringled or
even rough on palpation & the lesion can not be removed on
scrapping
Leukoplakia of the floor of the mouth has an ebbing-tide pattern
of appearance.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on METHOD.
short_answer.model_answer:
Ask the patient to indicate on the Line where the pain is in relation
to the 2 extremes.
Ask the patient to mark on the Line the point that he feels his
perception of his current state.
The VAS scare is determined by measuring in mm from the Left
hand end of the Line to the point that the patient marks.
When the VAS is properly explained to subjects, they can easily
understand its use and successfully use it to indicate his level
of pain response to hypersensitive stimuli.

short_answer.question: What are the types of MACROGLOSSIA?
short_answer.model_answer:
Macroglassia can be either congenital or acquired [secondary] in
nature
Relative Macroglassia - normal sized tongue appears abnormally
large, if it is enclosed in a small oral cavity.
It happens mostly in cases of maxillary retrusion or in cases of
restricted growth of nasopharynx
Apparant Macroglassia - inthis, tongue appears abnormally large due
to poor muscular control of the tongue, although there is no real increase
in tongue size.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of MACROGLOSSIA.
short_answer.model_answer:
Macroglossia is most prominent in infants but tongue size may remain
above normal in childhood and adolescence.
It cause displacement of teeth and malocclusion due To continuous pressure
on the Teeth by enlarged tongue.
Disturbs the process of speech or food intake
Lateral margin of tongue exhibits scalloping or Indentations
Blockage of the pharyngeal airway due to macroglossia may result in
a, condition called obstruction sleep apnea which is characterised by
intermittent cessation of respiration during sleep.
Edentulous patients with macroglassia often have difficulty in wearing
dentures.
Patient may complain of noisy breathing drooling of saliva

short_answer.question: Describe the management of MACROGLOSSIA.
short_answer.model_answer:
Orofacial therapy - it uses a palatal device to stimulate muscular
tone and proper tongue position IL
Surgical management - partial glassectomy is the Treatment of
choice.
Removal of the primary cause, wherever necessary at
Orthodontic treatment - correction of dental arch deformity &
malocclusion by arthodontic treatment
Speech therapy - correction of defective articulation by speech therapy

short_answer.question: What is the etiology of XEROSTOMIA?
short_answer.model_answer:
1> Radiation Induced - ionizing radiation to head and neck region for
the treatment of cancer results in pronounced changes in the salivary
glands Located within the primary beam.
The degree of damage caused by radiotherapy is related to dase-time-
volume factor.
Serous acinar cells are more sensitive to radiation, than the mucus
cells
Radiation sensitivity decreases in the following order

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Sublingual.
short_answer.model_answer:
2> Pharmacologically Induced - The classes of drugs which causes
xerostomia include anticonvulsants antihistamines, antiemetics,
antihypertensives.
3> Local factors - Decreased mastication, smoking, mouth breathing
can also Lead to xerastomia
4> Developmental - developmental abnormalities, tumors, autoimm-
-une states
5> Systemic factors - Diabetes mellitus, Sjogren's syndrome, IDA,
pernicious anemia

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Painful salivary gland enlargement.
short_answer.model_answer:
Effect on Oral Functions - increased thirst, increased uptake of
fluid especially while eating difficulty in swallowing, speech, and
eating dry food. There is also burning and tingling sensation in
the mouth, frequent oral infections, intolerance to dental
appliances and abnormal taste in the mouth.
Effect on Normal functions - hypofunction of other secretary
glands, blurred vision and ocular dryness, itching and burning
sensation in the eye. There is also dryness of pharynx and skin
Residual saliva is form foamy, thick and ropey.
Clinical signs - oral mucasa appears thin, pale and dry. Tongue blade
may adhere to soft tissues. Tongue may manifest deficiency by atrophy

short_answer.question: Write a short note on RISK FACTORS FOR ORAL CANCER.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> TOBACCO USE - Tobacco use is the major etiological factor for the
development of oral cancer.
The effect of tobacco use heavy alchohol consumption and poor diet
can probably explain 90% of cases of oral cancer.
Tobacco is used in 2 major farms
- smokeless tobacco
- smoking
Tobacco contains potent carcinogens Like nitrosamine, polycylic
hydrocarbons, polonium etc

Tobacco smoke contains CO, which is an important factor in the
development of oral cancer
2> ALCHOHOL - ALL forms of alchohol including hard Liquor, wine and
beer have been implicated in the etiology of oral cancer.
It acts by bits dehydrating effect on mucasa which increases the mucosal
permeability.
3> FAMILIAL AND GENETIC PREAISPOSITION - Though it is considered
as a risk factor, there is Little evidence supporting it.
4> ACTINIC RADIATION - It is a minor etiologic factor in case of lip
cancer
5> ORODENTAL FACTORS It is more prevalant in patients with
poor oral hygiene faulty restorations, sharp Teeth, ill-fitting
dentures and those with syphilitic glassitis
6> VIT. A DEFICIENCY - Vit. A deficiency can produce excessive
keratinisation of skin & HUCUS membrane and, thus may
predispase to carcinoma
7> IMMUNITY - The increasing incidence of oral cancers is clearly
age related, due to decreasing immune response with age.
It may occur in immunocompromised patients following
organ & bone marrow transplantation.
8> SYPHILIS - Oral malignancy is commonly found in Tertiary
stage of syphilis.
9> to IONISING RADIATION - Carcinoma of buccal mucosa may
occur as a complication of long term radiotherapy.
10> TRAUMA - Trauma in combination with other factors TOOD like миг chronic
check biting, denture use and irregularteeth may promote transforma-
-tion of epithelial cells.
11> VIRUS - Viruses such 0 as herpes simplex OF type 1, human papilloma
virus can be associated with oral cancer.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on NEWER APPROACHES.
short_answer.model_answer:
a> Photodynamic Therapy -it involves selective uptake & retention
of preadministered photosensitizer in a tumor. It is followed
by irradiation with LASER of specific wavelength.
It leads to necrosis of tumor due to formation of singlet oxygen
b> Intralesional chemotherapy - in this, drug is injected directly
inthe tumor
c> Intra-arterial chemotherapy - drug is injected into articries
supplying the tumor
d> Intensity Modulated Radiation therapy - it delivers specified
dasages of radiation therapy directly to cancer cells. This will
spare the surrounding healthy tissue and also contributes to
reduced xerastomia & reduced myopathy.
e> gene Therapy - it involves introduction of functional genetic
material into target cells to replace defective gene.

Intensifying screens converts X-Rays into multiple
visible rays [ Principle of phosphorescence.]
visible rays can range from violet to Green based
on the type of intensifying screen.
Forms image is less exposure.

viva.model_answer:
It is a common disease characterised by development of or painful, recurrant,
solitary or multiple ulcerations of the oral mucasa where the ulcers heal
spontaneously and there are no signs of any other disease.

It has been considered as an idiopathic disease and is associated with

viva.model_answer:
1> Minor Apthae - also called as canker sores in which the ulcers are
oval shaped, less than 1 cm in diameter, and heal
without scar
2> Major Apthae - also called as Sutton's disease or Periadenitis
mucasa necrotica recurrens and the ulcers are
oval shaped, more than 1 cm in diameter and heal
with scarring
3> Herpetiform ulcers - Recurrant crops of dozens of small ulcers which
are less than 0.5 cm are seen throughout oral mucasa

viva.question: Write a short note on ACCORDING TO SEVERITY.
viva.model_answer:

distinct ulcer-free periods
2> Complex Apthasis - Almost constant presence of 3 or more than 3
oral ulcers

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of ACCORDING TO SEVERITY.
viva.model_answer: It usually occurs in 2nd and 3rd decades of Life.

viva.question: Write a short note on Female predilection.
viva.model_answer:
It occurs most commonly in nonkeratinised mucasa such as buccal
and Labial mucasa tongue, soft palate, gingiva
Prodromal symptoms - prodromal burning itching for 24-48 hours,
before the ulcer appears.
The lesion is typically very painful, so it commonly interferes with
eating for several days
It begins as a single or multiple superficial erasions covered by gray
membrane.
It is surrounded by localised areas of erythema and develops within
hours.
Multiple lesions are present but the number and size varies.
The ulcers generally persist for 7-14 days, may recur after 1-4
months with no evidence of scarring.

viva.question: Describe the management of Female predilection.
viva.model_answer:
A> TOPICAL MANAGEMENT
Topical carticasteroid - 1% triamcinolone acetonide 3-4 times daily.
Other topical steroids are betamethasone syrup
and 0.01 % dexamethasone elixir.
Topical Anaesthetics - 2% viscous lidocaine, benzocaine and benzydamine
hydrochlaride may reduce pain.
Sucralfate - Topical application 4 times/day has shown soothing
effect on ulcer as it sticks to the mucus membrane.
Tetracycline mouthwash - 250 mg/30 ml used 4 times daily
Intralesional injection of sTeroid - Triamcinolone acten acetonide

Systemic therapy for 15 days followed by intralesional injection
every 2 weeks for 2 months reduce the recurrence
Long term steroid therapy should be avoided

viva.question: Write a short note on PREVALENCE.
viva.model_answer:
Submandibular ducts or glands - 80-94%
Paroted 5-18%
Sublingual n2%
The most common site is submandibular gland and Wharton's duct,
because its secretion is more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course & smaller orifice
It usually occurs in 3rd-5th decades, with slight male predominance
Simultaneous siablithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare
However, multiple sialoliths in the same gland or duct is common

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of PREVALENCE.
viva.model_answer:
Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the 2nd most common
salivary gland disease in children [ after mumps]
It is the major cause of unilateral, diffuse parotid or submandibular
gland swelling
Insome cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever.
occissociation
IN
with
Patients complain of pain and swelling during and after eating the
food when saliva production is at its peak. There is gradual
reduction of pain & swelling, which occurs at the resting period.
It recurs again repeatedly, when flow is stimulated.
Tenderness, trismus, decrease or absence of salivary flow, or
purulent discharge at the orifice of the duct may be present
Occasisionally, the sialolith presents no remarkable symptoms

viva.question: Describe the management of PREVALENCE.
viva.model_answer:
The treatment depends on the number, size and Location of sialolith.
If the sialolith is small and single, conservative management with
local heat, massage and sialogoques is attempted
In case of infection, initially antibiotics and anti-inflammatory kitaral
medication and enhanced use of sialogogues, salivary gland massage
and hyperhydration hoping for sportaneous passage is given
Techniques for removal of sialolith are -
A> Manual manipulation with the help of Lacrimal probes or dilators
[called as milking the gland ] - for sialoliths less than 2 cm, which
are Located peripherally near the ductal opening
B> Larger sialoliths in the anterior part are surgically removed,
by sialolithectomy, through a ductotomy, performed intraarally,
under LA. Posterior stones are removed under GA.
c> Ductal Cannulation

failed transoral route, require the removal of the gland.

Recurrent Herpetic stomatitis is usually seen in adult patients and

viva.question: Write a short note on PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
viva.model_answer:
Predisposing factors for reactivation include fever, upper respiratory
tract infection, Trauma and immunasuppression, fatigue, menstruation
and pregnancy, allergy or gastrointestinal disturbances.

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of PREDISPOSING FACTORS.
viva.model_answer:
Secondary herpes is the result of reactivation of Patent virus.
The viruses, once introduced in the body, appear to reside dormantly
within the regional ganglia, and when reactivation is Triggered, spread
along the nerves to the sites on oral thucasa and skin where they
destroy the epithelial cells and induce a typical nflammatory response.

viva.question: Write a short note on MUMPS.
viva.model_answer:
It is also called as epidemic parotitis.
Mumps is an acute contagious viral infection characterised by
unilateral or bilateral swelling of salivary glands usually the
parotid.
Other than salivary glands, it may also involve nerve, meninges,
pancreas, gonads.

viva.question: What is the etiology of MUMPS?
viva.model_answer:
It is caused by RNA virus which belongs to family paramyxoviridae
and is found in saliva and urine
It spreads by droplet infection or direct contact

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUMPS.
viva.model_answer:
Once transmitted through respiratory droplet saliva, urine or
fomites, the incubation period is usually 2-3 weeks.
It starts replicating in the respiratory epithelium and spreads to
Local Lymph nodes, and subsequently develops viraemia
The affected area shows perivascular and interstitial mononuclear
cell infiltrates with oedema
Necrasis of acinar and epithelial duct cells is seen in salivary glands
and in the germinal epithelium of seminiferous tubules.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of MUMPS.
viva.model_answer:
Predominant in childhood, more common in boys , age group 5-15 yrs
The disease is preceded by the onset of headache, chills, moderate
fever, vomitting and pain below the ear [ prodromal symptoms]
It is followed by firm, rubbery swelling of salivary glands, which
elevates the ear lasting for 1 week
Salivary gland involvement produces pain upon mastication
Pain and Tenderness may be severe during the rapid phase of
parotid enlargement, the swelling reaches its maximum in about
3 days, tremains at its peak for 2-3 days and then subsides
gradually.
Most cases are unrecognised because of the absence of parotid swelling
The submandibular gland may also be involved separately or
in conjunction with the parotid gland.

It is a measurement instrument that tries to measure a characte-

from none to an extreme amount of pain.

viva.question: Write a short note on METHOD.
viva.model_answer:
Ask the patient to indicate on the Line where the pain is in relation
to the 2 extremes.
Ask the patient to mark on the Line the point that he feels his
perception of his current state.
The VAS scare is determined by measuring in mm from the Left
hand end of the Line to the point that the patient marks.
When the VAS is properly explained to subjects, they can easily
understand its use and successfully use it to indicate his level
of pain response to hypersensitive stimuli.

viva.question: Write a short note on MACROGLOSSIA.
viva.model_answer:
Macroglassia is a retiv relatively common condition characterised by
an abnormally large tongue in the oral cavity.

viva.question: What are the types of MACROGLOSSIA?
viva.model_answer:
Macroglassia can be either congenital or acquired [secondary] in
nature
Relative Macroglassia - normal sized tongue appears abnormally
large, if it is enclosed in a small oral cavity.
It happens mostly in cases of maxillary retrusion or in cases of
restricted growth of nasopharynx
Apparant Macroglassia - inthis, tongue appears abnormally large due
to poor muscular control of the tongue, although there is no real increase
in tongue size.

viva.question: Describe MACROGLOSSIA.
viva.model_answer:
Congenital (BCD LMN H²V)
Acquired (A³MCE)

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of MACROGLOSSIA.
viva.model_answer:
Macroglossia is most prominent in infants but tongue size may remain
above normal in childhood and adolescence.
It cause displacement of teeth and malocclusion due To continuous pressure
on the Teeth by enlarged tongue.
Disturbs the process of speech or food intake
Lateral margin of tongue exhibits scalloping or Indentations
Blockage of the pharyngeal airway due to macroglossia may result in
a, condition called obstruction sleep apnea which is characterised by
intermittent cessation of respiration during sleep.
Edentulous patients with macroglassia often have difficulty in wearing
dentures.
Patient may complain of noisy breathing drooling of saliva

viva.question: Describe the management of MACROGLOSSIA.
viva.model_answer:
Orofacial therapy - it uses a palatal device to stimulate muscular
tone and proper tongue position IL
Surgical management - partial glassectomy is the Treatment of
choice.
Removal of the primary cause, wherever necessary at

viva.question: Write a short note on RISK FACTORS FOR ORAL CANCER.
viva.model_answer:
1> TOBACCO USE - Tobacco use is the major etiological factor for the
development of oral cancer.
The effect of tobacco use heavy alchohol consumption and poor diet
can probably explain 90% of cases of oral cancer.
Tobacco is used in 2 major farms
- smokeless tobacco
- smoking
Tobacco contains potent carcinogens Like nitrosamine, polycylic
hydrocarbons, polonium etc

Tobacco smoke contains CO, which is an important factor in the
development of oral cancer
2> ALCHOHOL - ALL forms of alchohol including hard Liquor, wine and
beer have been implicated in the etiology of oral cancer.
It acts by bits dehydrating effect on mucasa which increases the mucosal
permeability.
3> FAMILIAL AND GENETIC PREAISPOSITION - Though it is considered
as a risk factor, there is Little evidence supporting it.
4> ACTINIC RADIATION - It is a minor etiologic factor in case of lip
cancer
5> ORODENTAL FACTORS It is more prevalant in patients with
poor oral hygiene faulty restorations, sharp Teeth, ill-fitting
dentures and those with syphilitic glassitis
6> VIT. A DEFICIENCY - Vit. A deficiency can produce excessive
keratinisation of skin & HUCUS membrane and, thus may
predispase to carcinoma
7> IMMUNITY - The increasing incidence of oral cancers is clearly
age related, due to decreasing immune response with age.
It may occur in immunocompromised patients following
organ & bone marrow transplantation.
8> SYPHILIS - Oral malignancy is commonly found in Tertiary
stage of syphilis.
9> to IONISING RADIATION - Carcinoma of buccal mucosa may
occur as a complication of long term radiotherapy.
10> TRAUMA - Trauma in combination with other factors TOOD like миг chronic
check biting, denture use and irregularteeth may promote transforma-
-tion of epithelial cells.
11> VIRUS - Viruses such 0 as herpes simplex OF type 1, human papilloma
virus can be associated with oral cancer.

spare the surrounding healthy tissue and also contributes to
reduced xerastomia & reduced myopathy.
e> gene Therapy - it involves introduction of functional genetic
material into target cells to replace defective gene.

INTRODUCTION - These are the components in radiology to intensify the effects of

visible rays [ Principle of phosphorescence.]
visible rays can range from violet to Green based
